topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud. A single sentence, stark white against the usual cheerful formatting: "Please note that the surprise celebration for Sarah has been cancelled." My breath hitched. I stared at the screen, the pixels swimming before my eyes. A cold, heavy sensation settled in my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I started pacing the small living room, my footsteps echoing too loudly on the hardwood floor. I kept re-reading the message, searching for a typo, a mistake, anything to suggest this was a joke. The silence of the apartment suddenly felt cavernous, amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. I sank onto the sofa, my hands gripping the fabric so tightly my knuckles turned white. A sudden, hot sting pricked behind my eyes, and I squeezed them shut, trying to force the churning feeling down.

[story 2]

"Cancelled." The word echoed in my head, hollow and sharp. I reread the confirmation text three times, my thumb hovering uselessly over the screen. A strange pressure built behind my sternum, a tightening that made my shoulders hunch up toward my ears. I stood by the window, watching the streetlights blur through the damp evening air. Every sound—a distant siren, the hum of the refrigerator—seemed magnified, intrusive. My mouth went dry. I walked to the kitchen, my movements jerky and uncoordinated, and picked up a glass of water, but the liquid tasted like ash. I set the glass down with a clatter, the small noise seeming to shatter the fragile calm I was desperately trying to maintain. I felt a sudden urge to retreat, to simply disappear into the shadows.

[story 3]

I picked up my phone again, my fingers trembling slightly as I scrolled back through the invitation thread. The cheerful emojis, the planned decorations—all gone, replaced by that bleak notification. A wave of sheer paralysis washed over me. My vision tunneled slightly at the edges. I dropped the phone onto the counter; it didn't break, but the impact felt disproportionately loud in the quiet space. I began to rub my temples, a slow, repetitive motion that didn't bring any relief. My jaw felt clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles strain beneath my skin. I wanted to scream, to demand an explanation, but no sound escaped. Just a low, ragged exhale, and I leaned my forehead against the cool granite of the countertop, feeling utterly unmoored and adrift.
